definition: An observer whose approval must be shown through proper conduct and deference. The name suggests a deep-ocean elder being with a direct stake in the Chthonic's operation. Referenced by Captain William during his dressing-down of Olivia.

A Norse ritual offering to Aegir, god of the sea and its storms, referenced by Kara as part of a cross-cultural pattern linking coastal civilizations to bride offerings and binding rites. In Norse practice, the blot was a sacrificial feast made to the gods in exchange for their favor. Aegir hosted the gods at the bottom of the ocean, and the sea was understood as his hall.

The Neuroplex CEO. Predatory and gamified in his interest in the people around him, speaking of wanting something fresh. Travels on his own vessel and condescends to visit the Chthonic. In E4 he joins the ceremony as judge of Emma's readiness and loans Holly to Captain Dyer, noting the expense of keeping her current on her vaccinations. In E8 he dismisses Holly's prophetic ocean-speech and helps pitch the Naditu mythology to Mythos-core audiences. In E9, as the ship floods, he tries to pivot to an insurance claim and asserts his ownership stake before being purged.

House mother of the bachelorette party. Arrived for the Incognitoh game and stayed for the voyage. At the Tunngavik site she recognizes herself in the ancient carvings before she understands what that means. By E4 she speaks in the cadence of the ceremony. By E5 she is singing Mind Popper in a way that signals she is no longer operating on her own terms.

The nature of Ash is left deliberately unclear in early episodes. In E4 the named form surfaces: Ashur, the entity at the altar for whom Emma is being prepared. The name connects to Ashur, chief deity of the Assyrian pantheon, a war god and king of gods whose name gave rise to the word Assyria itself. In the show's cosmology, Ash is an Old One with a claim on a bride, and the Chthonic's entire operation exists in part to fulfill it.

A menacing hunt game. Players are directed toward hunting a target, with capture described as the win condition. Assets diagnoses the objectification built into a lot of game design, and foreshadows future action in the series.

A text Sarah finds in the Chthonic library, referencing a bride, a vessel, and an entity called the Old Groom. The passage she reads aloud describes a union that causes land to sink and seas to rise. It is the clearest statement in the arc of what the ceremony is actually for.

Subject. Coder. Between jobs. Designed addictive systems for a living and can name every mechanism being used on her. That knowledge has not protected her from any of it. She sees the architecture of the Valescape more clearly than anyone else on the subject side and cannot stop playing anyway. The Targeted Cycle is built around her.

Bowhead whale songs recorded and pitched up twelve octaves by Neural Nets and Pretty Patterns, producing a sound eerily close to human vocalization. Heard through the Chthonic's instrument room equipment. The show frames them not as ambient ocean noise but as intentional communication, something that wants to be heard. Selene describes them as guardians of the deep places. In E5 Kara tracks hundreds of them circling with no feeding pattern, no migration logic — just waiting. In E8 Kara's rave remix is laced with bowhead recordings. In E9 their song underlies the trifold resonance and surges through the corridors as the ship begins to flood.

Bridesmaid. Loud, present, and more perceptive than she lets on. She is among the first to admit she has been having the dreams. By E4 she participates in the ceremony as one of the chorus. By E5 she is singing Mind Popper in a way that signals she is no longer operating on her own terms. In E8 she and Tiffany recite the secret vow our purpose is service, service is our purpose during Holly's Naditu reveal.

Runs Midstream Sports. Holdfast adjacent, possibly without knowing it. Useful, earnest, and completely underestimated by everyone including himself. The sports podcast he runs with Hespa turns out to be the most consequential astroturfing operation in the arc.

A real naval disciplinary procedure, borrowed here as the name for the surveillance room where Captain William judges and reassigns crew. In the US Navy, captain's mast is non-judicial punishment administered by a commanding officer, outside the formal court-martial system. The show uses the term precisely: discipline that happens off the books, at the captain's discretion, with no appeal.

A cruise ship operating as an enclosed experimental environment, with guests unaware of the mechanisms operating around and beneath them. A wholly owned subsidiary of the Dagon Dream Group, operating under the authority of Neuroplex.

A fighting game whose characters are based on Vale Four subjects, built from their physical data including biometrics and body scans. Players control subject-based characters against each other. Blake recognizes Piper's character mid-match.

A substance Alastair administers to Holly as part of her conditioning regimen. He counts feeding it to her among the steps that should have ensured compliance. Despite the dose, Holly's resistance held when it mattered, a failure Olivia mocks him for. In E8 Alastair dismisses Holly's prophetic ocean-speech as more cognitolol talking.

June's offer to Blake in the Fletch scene. Described as measuring responses, but Naia clarifies it does not measure — it trains. Builds who you become. The suit is the mechanism behind the haptic conditioning tier of the Valescape.

A grouped set of episodes inside a Deep Dream State arc or season. Cycle titles and numbers are supplementary information. If you find them confusing, navigate by Season:Episode or Global Episode number instead.
The passenger-facing app for the Chthonic experience. Guests use it to summon crew via a bell icon, positioning control of the staff as a consumer convenience.

The corporate parent of the Chthonic. The name invokes the Lovecraftian sea deity Dagon, linking the cruise operation to the broader mythology of the Old Ones threading through Season 1. In E9 the rave is branded Ecstatic Awakening Night, sponsored by Dagon Dream Group, and Alastair invokes the company's insurance and track record of rebuilding as the ship begins to flood.

The genre originated by Deep Dream State. While conventional horror positions you against an outside threat, desire horror implicates you completely in it. The dread emerges from recognition, from understanding why the characters choose to stay, and why surrender ultimately feels like relief. The narrative treats desire as a structural force, built around documented psychological, sociological, and technological phenomena to engineer systems that exploit human wanting.

Holdfast. Performing bimbo while running legal strategy underneath. Went to law school. Topped her class. Has not forgotten a single thing they told her she forgot. Snapped back out of conditioning early and has been running a dual performance ever since — vapid affect on the surface, sharp legal mind underneath. The conditioning can break her mid-sentence if the right trigger fires.

The bride. Her wedding is the official reason for the Chthonic voyage and the nominal center of the bachelorette party, though the ship has its own reasons for having her aboard. She resists longer than most. By E4 the ceremony is built entirely around her, and the hesitation Captain Dyer flags as unreadiness is the last thing standing between her and whatever comes next. In E8 and E9 she takes part in the trifold resonance alongside Sarah and Mairead, helps Sarah reckon with the rave's commercialized aftermath, and welcomes the world that emerges once the ship goes under.

Chief engineer of the Chthonic. Practical, protective of Nikita, and the conscience of the crew quarters. Her relationship with Mairead is the emotional spine of the service deck. In E8 she resists the spa assignment given to Holly before Holly draws her into the Lotus, step by step, through a whispered sequence. In E9 her betrayal of Mairead, sharing the location of the hull's weak point, becomes the trigger for the ship's flooding.

Captain Dyer's word for Emma's life before the Chthonic found her. Used in a single dismissive sentence that implies she had a shop, a name, a fiance, a self. Whatever she was then is sealed off under that word. Olivia responds with whatevermore, and together they describe a door that has already closed.

Naia Anderson's underground yurt. This is where she plots her takeover of Vale Four and plans the resurgence of Meridian A. The interior is entirely ecological in design. The plants block signals and appear to be an extension of Naia herself.

Subject. Influencer. Arrived thinking this was a content opportunity and is not entirely wrong. Sees the shimmer on everything and reaches for it. Funny, perceptive, and very easy to target. Now an ongoing character by fan demand.
The total episode count for Deep Dream State across all arcs and seasons. A single number. Simple.

The reward system at the center of Nikki's conditioning. Stars are earned through obedience and worn as embroidered marks of status, reframing total compliance as achievement. Nikki describes them as the only thing that matters, and recalls crying the first time she was given one. In E8 gold stars are tied directly to Nikki's new uniform and to liquid praise. In E9 they are repackaged as rave merchandise, handed out by Nikki as limited-edition cruise-core maidwear.

Neuroplex systems engineer. Built the Valescape. Knows exactly what it does. Was placed inside Vale Four before it was Vale Four. Her sympathies have always been with the subversives. The avatars she designed to guide people through Snarecraft evolved beyond her intentions and became something she did not anticipate and cannot fully control.
Double agents operating inside the Neuroplex structure. They answer to Naia Anderson, the dispossessed company founder. Named after plant life, reflecting Naia's deep connection to botany.

Alastair's companion aboard the Chthonic. Presents as cheerful and compliant, but passes information in a dissociated state and her loyalty to Alastair is more complicated than it first appears. By E4 she participates in the ceremony and is loaned to Captain Dyer as a reward. In E5 she is the one who identifies Kara and Sarah as ideal vessels, noting their curiosity and obsessive need to understand as what makes them perfect prey. In E7 she leads Sarah and Mairead through a confession ritual in the ship's archive, framing surrender as revelation even as Selene intervenes. In E8 her Naditu conditioning reaches its endpoint: she is installed in the Lotus, marked with a glowing sigil, and delivers the Naditu reveal, the secret vow to betray those who doubted, timed to the ship's rave. She also draws Fion into the Lotus alongside her.

A university and affiliated podcast, the Hupakoē Herald, where Holly studied goddess myths and naditu lore before the Chthonic. Captain Dyer and Olivia reveal it as a long-running pipeline, a decade of funded lectures and symposia designed to plant the beliefs Holly now holds and believes she chose herself. The name is Greek for obedience.

What Kara declares herself and the chosen women to be at the Tunngavik site, distinct from and above the designation of vessel. The hybrid is not a container. The hybrid is becoming something that has not existed before.

Selene's name for the bowhead song heard during the rave sequence, distinct from the Hymn of R'lyeh. Not music in the conventional sense, but the actual mechanism by which the Old Ones communicate and bind. Kara's rave remix is laced with bowhead recordings, and the rite that follows, the trifold resonance, runs on this signal.

term: I'm not a person. I'm a pet.
definition: A pet-state phrase from E6, read aloud by Sarah as the book pushes her from personhood into trained obedience. The line marks the moment the episode's conditioning shifts from service into pettification.

The Mesopotamian goddess invoked in the confession ritual Holly leads in the ship's archive. Holly tells Mairead that Inanna absolves, framing the confession of guilt as the entry point into surrender. In E8 Sarah invokes Inanna again, this time as an affirmation of consent during the rave's trifold resonance.
A reality show game explored in Season Two of the Deep Dream State. The game uses surveillance not just to watch but to rewrite, turning visibility itself into the mechanism of control.

A hidden space aboard the Chthonic where deep ocean sound is monitored. Selene operates from here. It listens to the abyss, and the abyss responds. The bowhead recordings processed here are the clearest evidence that the ship's mission is not what passengers are told.

Neuroplex. Started reading copy. Became the ad. Became the game. Spotlight was built on her performance capture, her approval curves, her expression data. She moves between goddess, messenger, and subject — the only character in Vale Four who operates across all three registers. Believes she is the franchise. The arc does not entirely disagree.

Holdfast. Playing the loyal Neuroplex assistant while quietly working against everything she appears to serve. Brilliant at making compliance look like virtue. Her relationship with Z is eternal and genuinely affectionate and completely unresolvable. Whether she is loyal to him or steering him toward outcomes she has already decided on is a question the arc leaves open deliberately and permanently.

The internal currency used inside the Vale Four facility. Kamas are part of the isolated experiment structure, allowing the designers to rebuild the economy from scratch as another mechanism for engineering desire.

Bridesmaid and linguist with a deep research background in Lovecraftian mythology and deep ocean cosmology. On the Chthonic she moves from skeptical observer to active participant in the ship's hidden order. At the Tunngavik site she crosses a threshold no one else in the party is ready for. In E4 she is addressed by her changed name: Ka'ara. In E5 she and Sarah hole up in the ship's library, cross-referencing ancient texts and tracking the patterns that confirm the ceremony is designed, not spontaneous. In E8 she remixes the rave's soundtrack with bowhead recordings. In E9 she leads the trifold resonance with Selene, debates whether to fully open the gate to the Old Ones, and confirms the bulkhead fracture that floods the ship.

A ritual text sequence in the Chthonic's archive, found alongside the Hymn of R'lyeh and the Binding of Waters. Part of the seeded research Holly is led to discover, designed to escalate her sense of spiritual revelation and guide her toward the naditu cleanse.

The substance delivered through the Lotus during Holly's conditioning, distinct from the gold stars. Nikki administers it directly after tuning the Lotus's alignment curve, and Holly describes it as stroking her soul.

A conditioning chamber aboard the Chthonic, run in numbered modes such as Lotus Mode three. Holly is installed inside it for extended sessions and draws Fion in after her. By E9 the Lotuses are unlocked for Sarah, Emma, and Mairead during the rave's central ritual. Functions as both reward mechanism and binding device.

A title used in private for Captain William, distinct from his public-facing rank. It signals a hidden authority structure aboard the ship that operates separately from its cruise company cover. In E4 both Holly and Olivia address him directly as Magus during the ceremony.

Spa manager of the Chthonic's wellness center, and the crew member most visibly caught between survival and self-destruction. A skilled poker player with a gambling debt Alastair holds over her. Her assignment to guide the Tunngavik excursion is coercion dressed as opportunity. In E7 her guilt over what she has watched and participated in nearly pulls her into Holly's confession ritual before Selene calls a halt. In E9 she takes part in the trifold resonance, confronts Fion over her betrayal, then reveals the location of the hull fracture herself, choosing to let the ship flood.

Holdfast. The data scientist in the room. Documents everything and misses nothing. Her loyalty is to the methodology first, the mission second, and people a distant third. Presents as a comforting academic presence before the reveal. Her fascination with precision has made her the most assimilated person in the facility.

The original research operation founded by Naia Anderson before it became Vale Four. Naia retained a call option: if Vale Four's IPO fails or control is breached, Meridian A reverts to her automatically. The name is a pun. Its actual name, used by those who know it, is Meridiana.

A sports podcast that sponsors Vale Four. Run by Cael. Completely over the top. It frequently interrupts the action, and that's intentional. Deep Dream State doesn't do ads. Midstream is what we think of ads.

A Neuralverse series by Neural Nets and Pretty Patterns spanning mantric and game formats. Kara notes in E5 that Brittany and Amanda are singing Mind Popper in a way that signals they have crossed into compliance. The series functions across the Neuralverse as a sonic conditioning system, a recurring device that surfaces when a character is no longer operating on their own terms.

Hilton and Astoria, who frequently appear in opening scenes. Their names are derived from the feminine counterparts to Waldorf and Statler from The Muppet Show. They came for the Incognitoh game and appear to have stayed trapped in the system. These summaries are not found-footage contrivance. They are a built part of the plot.

Olivia's term for the fan audience the Naditu mythology is packaged for: lore-hungry consumers who treat the ship's belief system as aesthetic and erotic content. The Naditu reveal is built and marketed specifically for this audience.

In ancient Mesopotamia, the naditu were a class of consecrated women, typically associated with temples, who lived under religious restrictions and devoted themselves to service of a deity. The show reaches for this term at the Tunngavik site to name what is happening to the chosen women: not possession, not sacrifice, but a very old kind of dedication. The word carries weight across thousands of years of documented practice. In E5 the ritual logic surfaces again in the library texts Kara and Sarah read: through the cleansing waters, the vessel is stripped of memory, washed of self. In E7 Captain Dyer and Olivia reveal the term was seeded into Holly's education at Hupakoē long before the voyage, and Holly leads Sarah through a naditu cleanse chant in the ship's archive. In E8 Holly's Naditu conditioning culminates in the reveal: a secret vow to betray those who doubted, timed to the ship's rave.

Founder of Meridian A, the research operation that became Vale Four. Built the system before it had a brand. Was pushed out when they needed to scale it. Retained a call option that triggers automatically if the IPO fails. Has been waiting in the Garden Nest for someone credible enough to bring it down. Not a simple heroine. Does not particularly like people. Her relationship to the natural world is not metaphorical.

A surname Olivia catches herself using before correcting to Naylor. In Lovecraft's fiction, the nameless and the unnameable are structural features of cosmic horror, things too vast or wrong to be designated. Its appearance as a surname, nearly spoken aloud to passengers, suggests Olivia's identity is not entirely her own to give.

The sinister corporate entity at the center of the Neuralverse. Its first imperative is maximizing control and profit. People are a means to an end for Neuroplex, even if that end remains cloaked in mystery.

The lab Alastair references as the source of Holly's vaccinations, described as expensive. The name points toward Dulce Base, a persistent conspiracy theory about an underground government and alien research facility beneath Dulce, New Mexico. Grant Morrison's The Invisibles mapped similar territory as fiction. In the show's universe, New Mexico AC operates as a real institutional supplier to the Neuroplex network, connected to Wave Labs as part of the same infrastructure.

Ship maid of the Chthonic, introduced by Olivia as a demonstration. Her uniform is described as scandalous. She hears things no one else notices because no one locks up their secrets around the maid. Her whispered phrase after Room 203 — puppets program puppets — surfaces something the show will not let go of. In E7 she appears in surveillance playback as Nikki, fully conditioned, reporting on Holly's movements and reciting the gold star creed for Captain Dyer and Olivia. In E8 she administers liquid praise to Holly inside the Lotus and helps brand her with the Naditu sigil. In E9 she hands out gold-star rave merchandise as the ship's flooding begins.

Sarah uses the term to describe the physics of what the chosen women are becoming. In Lovecraft's fiction, non-Euclidean geometry is the signature of spaces that should not exist, angles that don't add up, architecture that the human mind cannot hold without breaking. The show borrows it precisely: the transformation is not metaphorical, it is structural.

Holly observes that Kara may speak it, cross-referencing ancient texts. In Grant Morrison's The Invisibles, a 64-letter forbidden alphabet functions as a psychic weapon, hijacking language processing and rewriting reality by restricting human thought. The show borrows the concept: some languages do not describe the world, they rewrite the person reading them.

The entity named in The Binding of Waters. The passage reads: the Old Groom shall claim his bride, through her the land shall sink, the seas rise, washed anew for the New Order. The name positions the cosmic horror not as a destroyer but as a bridegroom, and the ceremony not as sacrifice but as a wedding. In E7 Captain Dyer confirms that when the Groom calls again, Emma will open and answer, regardless of how much she remembers.

Ancient entities whose presence underlies the events of Season 1, framed by Captain William as the true owners of the space the crew inhabits. Selene references a coming reckoning in their name. The framework draws on Lovecraftian mythology while routing it through the mechanics of corporate power. In E7 Selene tells Sarah and the others that the Old Ones don't want to harm them, framing what's coming as deliverance rather than doom. In E9 Selene tells Sarah, Emma, and Mairead that the Old Ones remember them now, as the trifold resonance binds them together, and the ship begins to flood.

Cruise director of the Chthonic. The public face of the operation, managing guests while navigating the ship's hidden power structure. Nearly introduces herself as Olivia Nameless before catching herself. In E4 she co-officiates the ceremony and delivers the show's central creed without breaking stride. In E5 she agrees with Holly's assessment that Kara and Sarah's obsessive curiosity makes them perfect prey. In E7 she reviews surveillance playback with Captain Dyer, dresses down Alastair for the previous episode's failure, and oversees Nikki's conditioning report. In E8 she stage-manages Holly's Naditu reveal and the marketing of Naditu mythology to Mythos-core audiences. In E9 she hosts the Chthonic Company rave and loses control of it as the ritual and the flooding begin.

A secret vow recited by Tiffany and Brittany in call and response during Holly's Naditu reveal: our purpose is service, service is our purpose. Part of the same mantra family as We Know Our Place and Yes is best, marking characters who have moved past resistance into alignment.

Referenced by Kara alongside whale songs and forgotten gods as part of the constellation of real phenomena she and Sarah are tracking. Not yet fully explained, but placed alongside confirmed events. The pink fog connects to the NNPP phenomenon of reverse sapio, in which intelligence and awareness are inverted rather than suppressed, producing a state of heightened perception that is indistinguishable from dissolution. In E9 Emma distinguishes the rave's ritual, true rewriting, from the pink fog, framing what happened to her and Sarah as something more real than the fog's dissolution.

Subject. Teacher. Arrived with the most considered critique of choice architecture and is losing it game by game. Piper is vulnerable and defiant at the same time. Her subscription to liberal choice theory — the idea that choosing is itself protection from manipulation — is exactly what makes her easy to target.

A phrase from E6 where please becomes both desire and command. Sarah's request to please is turned into a reflexive need for instruction, showing how politeness, longing, and obedience collapse into the same pattern.

A conditioning phrase Olivia and Holly use to describe how Naditu conditioning works: subjects crave containment and train themselves to seek it without being told. Captain Dyer frames it as the easiest part of the system, a maze that runs itself once the gold stars are in place.

Kara reads aloud from a text describing patterns in behavior, songs that resonate with the blood, triggering obedience via sound waves, like an algorithm. The phrase lands as a direct in-world reference to the production methodology of Neural Nets and Pretty Patterns, making explicit what the show has always implied: the audio is the mechanism.

A clothing line described as mesh and light, and as scandalously revealing. Subjects who wear it become mapped data points. Ad messages appear directly on their bodies, turning them into billboards. The system adapts to feedback through mechanisms like the tingle clips.

Holly mentions that Emma is now handling six. The number is a measure of progress, of capacity, of how far the conditioning has taken hold. The term recurs across the Neuralverse as a unit of endurance and compliance, tracking how much a subject can take and still want more.

A phrase Nikita whispers in the crew quarters, unprompted, after her experience in Room 203. It surfaces the show's central interest in coercive systems that reproduce themselves through their subjects. Also the title of a Neuralverse companion track. In E5 Kara whispers it to herself in the library, reading about ritual washing and meat puppets.
A service game modelled on task management games. Players must serve an increasingly wide range of patrons. Purpose underlines the way games already trend toward power dynamics.

The sunken city from Lovecraft's mythology, whispered by Selene as the destination the bowhead whales are guarding. In the Cthulhu mythos, R'lyeh is where the Great Old One Cthulhu lies dreaming beneath the Pacific Ocean, waiting. The show places it in the Arctic, beneath the ice, close enough to touch from the Chthonic's excursion route. In E5 Kara's entire library research is oriented around it, tracking the signs of its awakening in real ocean data. In E7 a Hymn of R'lyeh appears among the seeded ritual texts Holly finds in the archive.

In Japanese horror culture, Room 203 carries specific weight. The number originates with Nanami Kamon's 2003 novel, about a woman driven to ruin by an inexplicable curse in her apartment, and expanded through internet folklore into the archetypal haunted room, the one where something went wrong and never stopped. Alastair's cabin aboard the Chthonic. Selene knows immediately where Nikita has been. In E7 Captain Dyer threatens to put another bed in Room 203 as a warning to Olivia.
A turn-based strategy game and Blake's game of choice. Included to make a point: getting smarter, or adding more design, is absolutely not the answer in a desire horror scenario.

Bridesmaid. The skeptic of the group, and the one Kara identifies as the smart one, for now. Her resistance at the Tunngavik site is the most articulate, and the most temporary. By E4 she speaks in the ceremony's register. In E5 she is in the library with Kara, cross-referencing ancient texts, and she is the one who follows the secret note alone. In E7 that note leads her, Holly, and the others through the ship's corridors into the archive, where Holly begins walking her through a naditu cleanse before Selene pulls her back. In E8 she is named as an easy mark for further conditioning. In E9 she takes part in the trifold resonance with Emma and Mairead, then helps Emma reckon with the rave's commercialized aftermath before the ship begins to flood.

Chief purser of the Chthonic, and its oldest crew member by her own account. She will be with the ship until it sinks. Keeper of the instrument room, where she monitors the deep ocean for sounds that travel for miles. She passes forbidden knowledge to passengers she judges ready for it, and speaks of a returning power that will change things when the time comes. In E7 she leads Sarah, Mairead, and Holly through the ship's corridors, confirms the bowheads are still circling, and intervenes when Holly's confession ritual pushes too far on Sarah. In E9 she leads the trifold resonance, reveals her own nature as a song given form by the Old Ones, and welcomes the flooding of the ship as the world that must change.

Seal-folk from Scottish and Irish mythology who shed their skins to walk as humans on land. In the lore, a selkie bride is made by stealing her seal skin: she cannot return to the sea without it, and so she stays, trapped in a human life by the thing that was taken from her. Kara names them as part of a cross-cultural pattern of coastal binding rites. The Chthonic's bachelorette party maps cleanly onto the archetype: women brought aboard, their old lives sealed off, unable to go back.

Vale Four's in-house professional development program, advertised by Iris and Meg. Marketed as income diversification and career advancement. Functions as a pipeline into deeper conditioning tiers and performance capture roles.

An Assistive Reality Game introduced in the Snarecraft ad at the end of the Targeted Cycle. Players track fugitives in real time for Kamas and access. The game blurs the line between entertainment and active surveillance participation. Its library mode contains real Vale Four documents that an intern accidentally included in the game assets.
A performance and attention game directly modelled on recurring character Iris Vale. Players compete for notoriety, trying to break out into fame and influence as they vie for the spotlight.

Automated service units, a fantasy construction of total automation in which human labor is replaced entirely by synthetic workers. Referenced by Fion as what Neuroplex's class of owner uses in place of human staff, making the crew's presence on the Chthonic a deliberate choice rather than a necessity.

A sacred ceremonial site on the island of Ra'iatea in French Polynesia, one of the most significant in all of Polynesian culture. It was the spiritual center of the cult of Oro, god of war and fertility, where high priests from across the Pacific gathered for ritual and political alliance. Human sacrifice was practiced here. Kara names it alongside the Aegir Blot and Selkies as evidence that the pattern of offering to sea powers is not mythology but history, repeated across every culture with a coastline.

Neuroplex. The public face of Vale Four's research program. Sadistic, precise, and genuinely good at her job. She means everything she says and that is what makes her dangerous. Presents as sympathetic before the reveal. Plays every character from a position of control, including her own.

A law of the deep invoked by Kara in E6. The phrase names the Chthonic power structure in miniature: every trap sits inside a larger trap, and every apparent controller can be answered by something deeper. In E7 surveillance playback opens with this line from Kara, echoing and distorted, before Olivia jokes that she thought she was the bigger tentacle.

A recurring countdown motif across the Neuralverse, used in Deep Dream State as the signal that a psychological threshold is approaching. Amanda's single tick, tock in the E4 ceremony is not punctuation. It is a summons. The motif dates to early catalogue releases and threads through multiple arcs as the sound the system makes when it is almost ready.

Bridesmaid. She stopped sleeping. She did not go to Tunngavik. In E7 her voice surfaces in surveillance playback, dreamy and barely conscious, echoing phrases like good girl and they're coming. In E8 she and Brittany recite the secret vow our purpose is service, service is our purpose during Holly's Naditu reveal.
A reward item Nikki requests by name from Olivia, part of the same conditioning economy as the gold stars. Olivia defers the request, promising to share later.

The ritual binding of bride, servant, and penitent, Emma, Sarah, and Mairead, during the rave in E8 and E9. Selene names it as the rite running underneath the Hymn of the Deep: real but temporary power, enough to break the ship's architecture for a moment.

A recurring Neuralverse term for pettification: training humans in an animal fashion through reward, command, repetition, and operant conditioning. In E6, Fion's "Say haiii, Trixie" marks Sarah's attempted reduction into a trained pet-state, just before Holly and Selene interrupt the handoff.

A real archaeological site in the Canadian Arctic, used as a shore excursion destination for Chthonic passengers. Olivia describes it as a place of ancient relics and lost knowledge. Its inclusion alongside the Old Ones mythology is not accidental, reaching toward something older than the cruise company's framing of it.
An arcade inside the Vale Four facility, used to help the research group map consumer choice. The gameplay is deeply addictive. There are rumors of depraved rooms deeper inside.

Selene describes certain places as locations where the veil is thin, where the boundary between the world passengers know and the older world beneath it becomes permeable. The Tunngavik site is one such place. The whales gather there. The veil is not a metaphor in the show's cosmology.

The designation Kara applies to the women chosen at the Tunngavik site before she revises upward. A vessel is a container. What the ancient ones want from the chosen women is not just containment. Kara draws the distinction herself: they are vessels, we are the hybrid. In E4 Captain Dyer asks Emma directly if she will be its vessel, and she says yes. In E5 Holly identifies Kara and Sarah as good vessels specifically because of their need to understand.

The game voice cast of the Valescape arcade. They voice the characters in Claws Out and other Vale Four games. Glitch Kitten (Aleice Eclipse), Joystick Jezbel (Kat McQueen), Power Up Peach (Brie Michelle), Pixel Dollie (Cherry Pak), Respawn Rouge (Kitty Pawa), Loot Bunny (Karma Reid).

A Neural Nets and Pretty Patterns production in which the ocean becomes the mechanism of erasure, washing away memory, resistance, and selfhood in successive layers. Captain Dyer's everything washes away sequence in E4 invokes it directly: thoughts are sandcastles, the old world is built on sand, and the tide is coming.

Part of the same institutional network as New Mexico AC, supplying the Chthonic operation. Where New Mexico AC handles biological preparation, Wave Labs operates in the acoustic and neurological space, developing the sound-based conditioning systems the ship deploys.

Olivia's creed, delivered in E4 during the ceremony: we know our place, we know our role, we know our need, we need control. A longstanding NNPP mantra that recurs across the Neuralverse, describing the internal state of those who have moved past resistance into willing participation in the system. It is not submission. It is alignment.
Mairead's official operation aboard the Chthonic. The gap between its name and function is doing a lot of work.

The entertainment act performing aboard the Chthonic. Their shanty references Wendy as a benevolent watcher who rewards passengers with tokens when the tide rises, a cheerful surface over something older.

Captain of the Chthonic, privately titled Magus. The name is a direct reference to the narrator of Lovecraft's At the Mountains of Madness. States he has been doing it for 144 years, suggesting he is not entirely human. In E4 he officiates the ceremony and sets the terms of Emma's transformation. In E5 Holly notes that the Dreamer may have a different dream in mind than Alastair's, signaling a divergence of interest within the ship's own power structure. In E7 he reviews surveillance with Olivia, disciplines Alastair over the previous episode's failure, and confirms that Emma will answer when the Groom calls again. In E8 he oversees Holly's Naditu reveal and the marketing pivot toward Mythos-core audiences. In E9 he insists he is still in control even as the ship floods, and Selene tells him he was never steering.

Brittany whispers it in the E4 ceremony: world beneath the world beneath the world beneath the... The phrase is the show's slogan and its cosmological premise. There is always another layer. The Chthonic operates on the surface of one, and the ceremony is an attempt to break through to the next.
A conditioning mantra from E6, whispered as part of the book's page-turn sequence. The phrase converts relief from stress into automatic agreement: no stress, just yes.
The karaoke act performing at the Chthonic's entertainment night. Their shanty is performed by the bachelorette party guests and serves as cover noise over the ship's actual business.

Referenced by Alastair as a future replacement for human secretaries, something that would make even a compliant Olivia redundant. The name suggests Yog-Sothoth, the Lovecraftian entity that is the gate and the key and the guardian of the gate, a being of pure cosmic knowledge. Applied here to a system that would absorb the administrative and compliance functions currently performed by conditioned humans.
A candy game with a suggestive emphasis on eating. It spawned one of the first secret tracks, featuring Pipp. You can explore the game further with the companion track.

The architect of Vale Four's operations. Runs the show and knows it. Never the one holding the leash, and always the one who decided where it goes. Presents as a vaudevillian producer type: witty, controlling, and genuinely entertained by what he builds.

The central subject of Violet. She enters therapy in a state of severe anxiety, haunted by memories of the Pink Room and convinced that her past is visible to everyone around her. Her panic, grounding exercises, showroom flashbacks, and ritual integration make her the first major subject of the Incognitoh arc. Violet's fear is not simply treated by the system; it becomes the material the system uses.

Violet's therapist at Deep Dream Wellness. She presents her work through clinical progress notes, diagnosis language, grounding exercises, and reassuring therapeutic control. As the episode unfolds, Lilly's role shifts from treatment provider to ritual participant. Her office practice reveals itself as part of the same apparatus Violet fears.

A ritual guide and antagonist figure in Violet's grounding sequence. Bella mocks Violet's anxiety, reframes the Pink Room as fantasy, and insists that the ritual landscape is real. She calls Violet Chloe, turning a name into a category of weakness and performance. Her function is to convert therapy language into humiliation, control, and instruction.

A chorus figure in the Violet ritual sequence. Candi leads and echoes the threefold chant, reinforcing the movement from therapeutic counting into collective ritual. Her stage-whispered responses make the ritual feel coordinated, rehearsed, and already underway before Violet understands it.

The apparent therapy office where Violet receives treatment from Lilly Ekimmu. The space presents itself through clinical notes, diagnosis codes, grounding exercises, and anxiety management. Its calm surface is part of the trap. The episode reveals the office as a therapeutic front for deeper conditioning.

The room Violet remembers as the source of her fear, shame, and showroom bleedthrough. It is associated with the dream machine, lotus clothes, tokens, cameras, collar marks, and Ash. Violet experiences it as something that follows her into public life. Ordinary spaces become extensions of the Pink Room once she believes everyone can see what happened there.

The watched space Violet keeps returning to in memory and panic. She hears locks, cameras, tokens, whispers, and laughter. She believes the people around her can see what happened there. The showroom turns exposure into the mechanism, making fear itself part of the performance.

A grounding exercise used on Violet. The pattern asks for three things seen, three things heard, and three body sensations. In Violet, the technique begins as anxiety management and mutates into a ritual structure. It carries the episode from therapy office to stones to whorship to final integration.

A therapeutic word that changes meaning over the course of Violet. At first, being grounded means staying calm and oriented in reality. By the ritual sequence, it means being placed in the body, placed on the grass, and placed inside the circle. The safety technique becomes a way to make Violet participate in her own containment.

The ritual designation for Ash's chosen girls in Violet. Bella describes the women on the stones as lucky, best, and offered. Lilly later repeats the logic herself, making the offering not just an image Violet sees, but a status the episode is moving her toward.

Bella's name for the ritual in Violet. The term collapses worship, exposure, bodily performance, shame, and devotion into one word. It marks the point where Violet's grounding exercise fully becomes a collective rite.

A device associated with the Pink Room and Violet's altered memories. Violet hears its waves during grounding and later recalls being strapped to it. The machine functions as both clinical object and dream apparatus, blurring treatment, programming, and fantasy.

The escalation named in Lilly's final progress note for Violet. It reframes the episode's therapy, ritual, and integration as archive material inside a deeper institutional layer. The phrase suggests that Deep Dream State is not only the show's title, but also an in-world status or process.

Bella's dismissive category for Violet during the ritual sequence. Chloe is not treated as a name so much as a type: anxious, self-conscious, eager to narrate her suffering, and easy to redirect. The term turns Violet's identity into a role she can be mocked into occupying.

A threefold naming sequence chanted during Violet's ritual. The phrase turns Violet's name into a progression of identities, moving from person to texture to performed creature. It mirrors the episode's larger pattern of therapeutic counting becoming ritual transformation.

The transparent competition space in Glass Houses. The Glass House makes privacy conditional, visible, and gamified: contestants can control who sees them, but using privacy too often becomes suspicious. Its architecture turns the ordinary act of living together into surveillance, strategy, and performance.

A contestant's hidden Incognitoh identity. Each player chooses an icon and must keep it secret while trying to identify everyone else's. The secret icon turns personality, preference, and self-presentation into clues the other contestants can use against them.

Synthetic service entities used by Neuroplex and its associated systems. In Glass Houses, April identifies herself as a house synthserv: an automated helper built directly into the Glass House. She manages comfort, rules, visibility, and game flow while presenting control as service.

The radial room arrangement inside the Glass House, described by Kitty as the sleepy circle. Its dollhouse structure makes private life part of the game architecture. Each contestant's room functions as both personal space and surveilled performance cell.

Kimberley's self-conscious social label in Glass Houses, tied to age, femininity, embarrassment, and the way the other contestants read her. Her attempt to play into the joke by choosing the bottle icon makes her easier to identify. The label turns a stereotype into a game liability.

A diminutive label used by the Council while observing the Glass House. The word frames obedience as identity, performance, and visible status. It turns compliance into something watched, judged, and praised.

A Glass House competition in which contestants try to earn protection from exposure and elimination. In Glass Houses, the immunity challenge requires players to perform privately while remaining identifiable only to themselves. The game makes silence, recognition, and embarrassment part of the mechanics.

A reward that protects a player from exposure by allowing a reshuffle. If a contestant's old icon is identified, the immunity token can erase the disadvantage by changing the game state. It is both a prize and a temporary escape hatch.

The state of being correctly identified in Incognitoh. A contestant is exposed when the other players connect their name to their secret icon, or when a player correctly stops an immunity challenge performance. Exposure turns secrecy into public loss and makes visibility the game's central punishment.

The house synthserv and game authority inside the Glass House. April manages the rules, sessions, screens, privacy states, and contestant flow while presenting herself as helpful and neutral. Her authority is soft in tone but hard in function, making her the voice through which the house converts service into control.

A Glass House contestant whose barista persona makes her social, flirtatious, and easy to underestimate. Her line, what can I get started for you, carries over into the game as both cover and clue. By Uniforms, Hannah is using intimacy, alliance, and performance as strategy, even as the uniform system starts using her back.

A Glass House contestant and developer who tries to read the game as a system before it can read her. She is skeptical, strategic, and quick to notice when mechanics hide inside social pressure. Her confidence in her own intelligence makes her dangerous to the game, and vulnerable to it.

A Glass House contestant who treats performance, dominance, and strategy as the same skill set. She is theatrical, competitive, and increasingly convinced she can control the people around her. Uniforms positions Madison as someone trying to run the show while the show quietly learns how to run her.

A Glass House contestant and gamer whose competence makes her an immediate target. She understands game logic, asks the right questions about the uniforms, and keeps playing even after the feedback starts changing her. Her ability to win becomes the very channel through which the system reaches her.

A previous Incognitoh winner who now functions as both mascot and mechanism. Kitty tests the uniforms, feels what the contestants feel, and translates system control into cheer, flirtation, and reward language. She presents as silly, but her silliness is part of the machinery.

The eliminated contestant whose breakdown becomes a warning to everyone still inside the Glass House. The others use her exit as gossip, strategy, and social distancing, proving how quickly embarrassment becomes currency in Incognitoh. Her fate shows the players what exposure looks like before they understand how close they all are to it.

The collective watcher-voice behind the Glass House. The Council observes contestants, comments on their reactions, and reinforces the game logic from behind the visible host layer. Its presence makes the house feel less like a show with rules and more like a system with appetites.

The visual identity markers contestants choose and hide inside Incognitoh. Icons turn personality, desire, embarrassment, and self-presentation into readable game objects. Once an icon is connected to a player, the secret self becomes public leverage.

The Glass House reward currency tied to advantage, protection, and continued play. Tokens promise agency, but April warns contestants not to depend on them because they run out fast. The system uses tokens to make scarcity feel like choice.

A strategic bond between contestants inside the Glass House. Alliances promise safety and shared information, but they also create new channels for betrayal, leverage, and seduction. In Incognitoh, trusting someone can be just another way to become visible.

The required visual layer attached to a contestant's uniform and icon identity. April insists that the uniform must be worn with all of its iconwear, making the hidden game marker part of the body. Iconwear turns secrecy into costume and costume into evidence.

Kitty's cute name for the physical feedback produced by the uniforms. The buzzies reward play, progress, and compliance through sensation, making the game feel good before the contestants understand why. The language disguises a serious control mechanism.

Kitty's enhanced uniform, which receives feeds from the other contestants' uniforms. Because she tests all the uniforms, Kitty can feel what the players feel and becomes a living hub for the feedback system. The super uniform makes her both winner and infrastructure.

A desired reward or access state referenced by the Council during the uniform sequence. Contestants and listeners are encouraged to want it, but Bella says MROs belong to the Inner Circle. The term marks a higher tier of the game, close enough to motivate players and distant enough to keep them reaching.

A higher access tier inside the Incognitoh system. Bella identifies MROs as an Inner Circle thing, implying that ordinary contestants can approach the reward without possessing it. The Inner Circle gives the game a ladder above the visible ladder.

term: Good Girls Make More Good Girls
definition: A Neuralverse rule stated by Kitty at the end of Uniforms. The phrase describes how conditioning reproduces itself through the already-conditioned, turning participants into trainers for the next subject. In the Glass House, it explains why Kitty's help is never just help.

The hidden store of player data, profiles, footage, and system records inside Incognitoh. Zara accesses the archive believing she has hacked her way into an advantage, using it to study contestants, judges, and the game itself. The episode reveals that this access was anticipated and permitted by the system, turning the archive from a secret weapon into another trap.

A uniform-linked control mechanism used to condition contestants through physical feedback. In Deep Fake, Zara identifies data profile and tingle control as part of how the uniforms make agreement, compliance, and self-rewriting feel easy. The term makes clear that the game is not only watching behavior, but actively shaping bodies and preferences through sensation.

The punitive version of the uniform feedback signal. April describes the bad buzz as a serious game consequence, tied to exposure, elimination, and the risk of game over. The childish phrasing softens the threat while making the punishment feel like just another cute rule inside the Glass House.

Former Incognitoh winners who return as part of the show's machinery. Kitty and Candy appear as examples of winners who remain inside the system, helping explain rules, model outcomes, and reinforce the idea that there is always another winner. Their presence makes victory look less like escape and more like recruitment into the game's ongoing structure.

The identity Zara is reduced to after her attempted hack is exposed. Once the system broadcasts her private performances and reframes her public identity, Zara is no longer treated as the developer in control, but as Program Puppy. The name collapses coding, pettification, and public humiliation into one role, showing how thoroughly the system has rewritten her leverage.

A Deep Fake variation on the show's recurring puppet logic. The phrase names the feedback loop where contestants try to program one another while being programmed themselves. In this episode, Zara and Madison both believe they are using control as strategy, but the system converts that belief into the very mechanism that exposes them.

The final-round challenge in Winner Winner. Contestants must move lollipops into their bowls while the room prevents ordinary upright movement. The game turns crawling, humiliation, speed, and alliance strategy into the same mechanic. Its apparent simplicity hides the fact that the contestants are competing inside rules designed to make control feel impossible.

The Hannah and Ashley pairing inside the late Incognitoh game. Hashley is both an alliance and a romanticized strategy, giving Ashley a reason to trust Hannah against Madison. Hannah uses that trust to consolidate power while still framing the move as shared survival. The name makes the relationship feel playful even as the game turns it into leverage.

Madison's imposed identity during the final Incognitoh phase. Hannah names Madison Mimi and presents the collar as if the role has already been printed into the game. The name reduces Madison's dominant strategy into a pet-state assignment she has to compete within. Mimi mirrors Trixie, turning rivalry into a contest over who can submit better.

A Neuroplex-linked coffee brand surfaced through Hannah's reset uniform. April describes Bimbucks as a distinct legal entity, suggesting the brand belongs to the same corporate ecosystem as the Glass House. Hannah's old barista coding becomes literal wardrobe, making her employment history into a public game object. The brand ties service work, scripted friendliness, and compliance into one pink commercial surface.

The reset mechanism that assigns Madison's new uniform after she cashes in her immunity tokens. Instead of letting her choose a new icon, the game uses psychographic profiling to decide what role fits her. The formula turns personality data into costume, making the contestant's hidden history more powerful than her stated preference. It marks the shift from player choice to predictive control.

Ashley's final-phase identity in Winner Winner. After the reset, her uniform adds horns, a tail, a collar, and a bell, with the unlocking mechanism requiring a specific sound prompt. Ashley names the result herself: time to be a moo moo. The phrase turns the secret icon into an embodied role, making the final phase less about hiding and more about becoming visible.

April's name for the final phase of the Incognitoh game. Earlier rounds depend on secret icons and strategic concealment, but Winner Winner shifts toward forced revelation. Ashley repeats the phrase as she realizes her icon is no longer hidden. The line marks the moment when the game stops rewarding secrecy and starts rewarding exposure.

term: What Can I Get Started For You
definition: Hannah's service phrase and identity anchor. Bella demands that Hannah say the line during her Bimbucks reset, turning the phrase from customer-service habit into a triggered performance. It connects Hannah's barista persona to the broader Neuralverse logic of service, compliance, and scripted desire. The line works because it sounds ordinary until the system makes ordinary speech into instruction.

The final challenge of Incognitoh. Each remaining contestant is linked to another through an interconnected system of stimulation and reward, so one player's reaction becomes another player's clue. The challenge forces contestants to watch one another with total intensity, turning expression, voice, posture, and involuntary response into game data. It is the endpoint of the Glass House logic: privacy is gone, the masks are coming off, and even sensation has become communal.

A new advertised setting introduced during Madison's post-victory exposure sequence. The ad frames it as a place with a new boss, a new gang, and a new wave, escalating Incognitoh's logic into a broader urban game world. Its language suggests both competition and infiltration, asking whether the viewer can beat it, get inside it, and get Liquid Praise. Niku City reads as another Neuroplex entertainment surface where game, body, and reward system collapse together.

A reality-show format advertised after Madison claims the prize. The Island promises adventure, paradise, friendship, rivalry, and the ultimate challenge. Its pitch sounds like a cheerful survival competition until it reveals the real target: triumphing over reality itself. The Island extends the Incognitoh model outward, turning ordinary reality-show stakes into another Neuroplex structure for rewriting contestants.

A Neuroplex-linked investigative service advertised in the closing sequence of The Chain. The ad presents Neuro Discovery as a detective operation for missing persons, corporate investigations, and hidden truths. Its promise of state-of-the-art technology and old-fashioned intuition sounds reassuring, but in the context of Incognitoh it reads as another surveillance product. The slogan, your mystery is our mission, turns discovery itself into a corporate service.

The research institution at the center of the Sitri arc. Sitri presents itself as a dream-therapy and lucid-dream research facility, but its work quickly moves from observation into behavioral architecture. The Center is not just a place where dreams are studied; it is a system that learns how to use dreams as structure, leverage, and control.

Tessa Finn's Sitri research discipline. Applied Dream Engineering treats dreams as systems that can be analyzed, altered, and rewritten rather than simply interpreted. Its therapeutic language makes the work sound clinical and constructive, but the episode immediately shows how easily engineering a dream becomes engineering the dreamer.

The room where Sitri subjects sleep, dream, and become readable to the research team. Its soundscape is defined by controlled hum, medical stillness, and binaural atmosphere. The chamber appears passive at first, but it functions as the physical interface between subject, dream, and institutional control.

Tessa's mastery prototype for Phoebe Bosworth's first intervention trial. Construct 37 is designed to disrupt recursive shame loops and guide Phoebe toward an altered response pattern. The construct begins as therapy, but its failure to control the dream reveals the central danger of Sitri: the dream is not only being rewritten, it is starting to write back.

The clinical framework Tessa uses to explain Phoebe's embarrassment dreams. In her reading, the dream rehearses fear so the mind can master it. Meg challenges that optimism, arguing that Phoebe's subconscious is not rehearsing resilience but repeating a reward loop. The disagreement names the episode's first major fault line: therapy versus appetite, diagnosis versus desire.

The name imposed on Phoebe inside the classroom dream. Cael uses it while directing her to the blackboard, reducing Phoebe's adult identity into a smaller and more performable role. The name marks the dream's shift from anxiety scenario into behavioral script. Fifi is not simply a nickname; it is an early sign that the dream can rename the subject.

A sponsored mindfulness app advertised before the first Sitri session. Better Self promises calm, habit reshaping, sleep focus, and evidence-based audio support. Its language mirrors Sitri's clinical optimism while quietly introducing the arc's core idea: self-improvement becomes most dangerous when the self is treated as editable software.

A meditation product advertised in the middle of Rehearsal. Please Space promises to guide the listener away from noise and into the silence they crave. Its language moves from wellness into command, especially through the phrase reset, rewire, rewrite. Like Better Self, it presents surrender to structure as relief.

The Please Space slogan and one of the Sitri arc's cleanest statements of method. Each verb sounds therapeutic in isolation, but together they describe escalating control over the mind. Reset removes the current state, rewire changes the underlying pathways, and rewrite replaces the script. The phrase turns wellness branding into a procedural outline for transformation.

Zev's teasing name for Tessa in Rehearsal. The phrase connects Tessa's competence, ambition, and susceptibility to reward into one compact label. It also echoes the broader Neuralverse reward economy, where praise and achievement often become mechanisms of control. In the Sitri context, gold star girl is affectionate on the surface and diagnostic underneath.

Cael's Sitri presentation appears inside the dream as a teacherly and ritualized presence. They speak with calm authority, turning classroom structure into instruction and humiliation into procedure. This version of Cael is less a conventional guide than a sign that the dream has begun generating institutional figures of its own. Their voice marks a crossing deeper into the system.

Hespa's Sitri presentation functions as a clinical handoff voice inside the dream. They deliver information, correction, and reinforcement with almost no emotional disturbance. In Rehearsal, Hespa helps convert Phoebe's fear into a staged lesson, making the dream feel administered rather than accidental. This version of Hespa belongs to the machinery of transition and compliance.

Iris Vale's Sitri presentation begins as the sponsored voice of wellness. She delivers Better Self and Please Space copy with warmth, smoothness, and a cadence that feels too intimate to be merely commercial. In this arc, Iris seems to operate from behind the veil of the institution, half advertisement and half protocol. Whether she is person, brand, ghost, or system remains deliberately unstable.

June Lowell's Sitri presentation is the disciplinary intelligence of the research team. She is precise, controlled, and openly impatient with optimism that outruns methodology. June does not need to raise her voice to dominate the room; her authority comes from certainty and institutional alignment. In Rehearsal, she frames the experiment as a matter of rigor, validation, and outcome control.

Meg Aerin's Sitri presentation is rational, combative, and intensely guarded. She challenges Tessa's therapeutic optimism by arguing that Phoebe's dreams are not maladaptive rehearsals but escalating reward loops. Meg's skepticism makes her seem protected, but the episode also shows how easily her pride, rivalry, and appetite can become part of the experiment. She enters as the critic of the system and already sounds like one of its instruments.

Tessa Finn's Sitri presentation is an applied dream engineer and true believer in therapeutic intervention. She wants to reshape fear into mastery and insists that Phoebe's dreams can be guided toward control. Her confidence is sincere, but the episode immediately places that confidence under pressure from Meg, June, Zev, and the dream itself. Sitri Tessa is not Vale Four Tessa; this version still believes the machine can heal because she has not yet understood what else it wants.

Zev Talcott's Sitri presentation is a corporate operator wrapped in fake-casual charm. He downplays titles, softens conflict, and makes control feel like personal attention. His presence reveals that Sitri is not just a research project but part of a larger infrastructure of performance, funding, and behavioral leverage. Zev rarely needs to issue a direct order because the room is already shaped around what he wants.

A dream figure whose identity appears unstable and may shift names across the Sitri arc. Nyra speaks in soothing, insistent affirmations that blend meditation, programming, and praise. She does not feel like a single person so much as a recurring voice pattern, an echo of a deeper institutional presence inside the dream. When Nyra speaks, the system sounds as if it has learned how to comfort and command at the same time.

Sitri's first active subject in Rehearsal. Phoebe's dreams center on visibility, embarrassment, and the fear that everyone can see what she is trying to hide. Tessa reads her case as a chance for therapeutic mastery, while Meg sees an escalating reward loop the intervention is unlikely to control. Phoebe becomes the first proof that the dream is not simply a symptom to be treated; it is a structure that can answer back.

The Sitri room where researchers watch, argue over, and interpret subject response in real time. Unlike the Dream Chamber, which holds the subject's sleeping body, the Observation Chamber is where institutional judgment takes shape. In GE17, the room becomes the site where Phoebe's dream data turns into professional accusation, political leverage, and proof of Tessa's failure.

The leadership role and research phase Tessa loses after the Construct 37 trial. The title suggests Sitri's work is meant to move beyond intervention into active synthesis, creating dream structures rather than merely responding to them. Tessa's confession frames the position as the thing she wanted before the experiment exposed what she wanted even more.

June's term for what begins happening to Phoebe once the Construct 37 intervention fails. The subject is no longer simply reacting to fear, shame, or exposure; she is bonding to the pattern itself. Imprinting marks the shift from dream content into behavioral installation, the point where a repeated response becomes part of the subject's structure.

A dream-state role imposed on Phoebe during the GE17 escalation. The phrase reduces her from subject to experimental animal, turning sensors, observation, and compliance into part of the dream's identity script. In Sitri logic, lab rat is both insult and diagnosis: the subject has become the thing the institution was already treating her as.

Elle Lawson's Sitri presentation is Subject L-Zero-Two, a sleep-paralysis subject whose dreams are unusually receptive to the Center's protocols. Unlike her Vale Four presentation, this version of Elle is still inside the dream architecture rather than strategically operating around it. Her paralysis, dream recall, and responsiveness make her valuable to Meg's research, but also make her vulnerable to being treated as evidence before she is treated as a person. Sitri Elle is not merely observed by the system; she begins to synchronize with it.

Elle Lawson's subject designation inside the Sitri Center. The label places her within the Center's clinical indexing system, reducing her name and history to a research marker. In Incubator, the designation frames her persistent REM paralysis as a case study rather than a crisis. It also signals that Elle is part of a broader subject cluster, not an isolated anomaly.

The subject grouping Meg uses to contextualize Elle's case. The cluster appears to involve invasive dream presence, early-stage conflict, and confusion after waking. By placing Elle inside the Forty-Four cluster, Meg treats mythic sleep-paralysis imagery as repeatable research data. The term matters because it turns folklore, fear, and bodily response into a classification system.

Meg's term for Elle's full REM paralysis state. Sleep-lock describes a body that cannot move while the dream remains active, receptive, and measurable. In Sitri logic, immobility is not only a symptom but an experimental condition. The more locked the subject becomes, the more the dream can be studied, shaped, and used.

Meg's name for the accelerating pattern inside Elle's dream response. The loop turns fear, recognition, repetition, and attachment into a self-reinforcing structure. What begins as a threatening dream presence starts to behave like a ritualized return. The phrase is important because it shows Sitri moving from therapy language into worship language while pretending the distinction still holds.

The Sitri term for dream influence that carries through into waking speech or behavior. Meg uses it when Elle wakes after speaking just before surfacing. Residual suggestion implies that the dream is not sealed off when the subject opens her eyes. Something lingers, and the Center is interested in measuring exactly how much.

Visual Induction of Lucid Dreams, introduced by Meg as part of Elle's training. VILD asks the subject to build the dream deliberately through repeated imagined scenes, objects, rooms, voices, and scripts. Meg presents it as a method for control, but the episode makes that claim unstable. Once the subject builds the script, the performance may continue without asking permission.

Mnemonic Induction of Lucid Dreams, introduced by Meg as the phrase-based half of Elle's training. MILD uses repetition to install a memory cue before sleep, so the subject can recognize the dream while inside it. Meg describes it almost like a prayer, turning clinical induction into ritual practice. The method fits Sitri's larger pattern: language repeats until it becomes architecture.

The writing practice Meg assigns Elle after introducing VILD and MILD. Elle is told to record every dream immediately after waking, even if the entry is fragmentary or strange. The journal is framed as recall training, but it also teaches the subject to treat the dream as important, repeatable, and institutionally legible. In Sitri, even private memory becomes paperwork.

Elle's recurring name for one of her sleep-paralysis dreams. The dream involves silver strings and the sense that her movements are being controlled by something outside herself. Meg treats the dream as useful data because it gives Sitri a clear grammar of surrender, agency, and response. The puppet dream connects Elle's case to the wider Neuralverse pattern of systems that make control feel like self-discovery.

A word introduced in the Please Space ad during Incubator. It appears inside the ad's soothing rhythm, positioned near the promise of silence, sleep, and surrender to guided stillness. The term evokes mythic and ritual language without fully explaining itself, which makes it feel less like a product feature than a signal. In Sitri, even the ad copy may be part of the dream layer.

A dream-name applied to Tessa during Elle's GE19 dream sequence. Nyra calls her over with the name, and Tessa responds instantly, making the label feel less like teasing and more like a role she already knows how to occupy. Teehee marks Tessa's shift from failed researcher into dream-function, helper, trainer, and example. The name is childish on the surface, but in Sitri logic it signals successful reduction into a usable script.

Tessa's explanation for Elle physically reenacting dream movement in the Dream Chamber. The phrase makes a serious boundary failure sound like a routine technical artifact. By labeling the event kinetic spill, Tessa protects Elle from scrutiny while also normalizing the dream's leakage into the body. It is one of Sitri's cleanest examples of institutional language making loss of control sound manageable.

Tessa's private dream phrase in GE19. She repeats it before the mirror until her voice no longer feels like her own. The line reframes forgetting as preparation rather than damage, making erasure sound chosen, practiced, and even professional. In Sitri, the phrase functions as both induction and confession.

A Sitri condition in which a subject is deeply under and past ordinary REM thresholds. Tessa uses it to describe Meg's state after drop-in, making clear that Meg is not simply asleep but sealed into the dream process. Dreamlocked is related to sleep-lock, but it carries a stronger sense of containment inside an active dream environment. The term marks the moment when the chamber is no longer observing a subject from outside the dream; the subject is trapped inside the operating layer.

The failed research grouping named in Meg's GE19 confession. The phrase gives Sitri's collapse a formal institutional label, turning the events around Elle, Meg, and Tessa into an internal review category. Dream Cohort 3-Beta suggests the Center is running multiple dream-subject groupings with numbered phases, benchmarks, and failure states. It also makes the personal disaster sound like a managed research incident.

The Sitri program Meg says she led before her demotion. The phrase names an early formal layer of the Center's work, focused on lucid dreaming, inducement, and behavioral conditioning. Meg presents herself as its former Lead Coordinator, which makes her fall especially pointed: she is consumed by the system she was supposed to manage. Phase-One Lucid Conditioning establishes that Sitri's dream work is organized as a staged institutional pipeline.

A research marker Meg admits to falsifying in her confession. The term suggests dream-state crossover, where one subject's material, response, or imagery appears to bleed into another's. In GE19, induced overlap becomes part of Meg's attempt to regain control by manipulating records and making Tessa look unstable. It is both a technical Sitri concept and evidence that the institution's data can be rewritten by the people trapped inside it.

Z's preferred framing for Meg's punishment and demotion. He treats alignment as a positive indicator, implying that a subject or researcher is becoming consistent with the dream protocol's desired outcome. The word is softer than coercion, cleaner than obedience, and more useful in a report. In Sitri language, alignment turns surrender into a metric.

The outcome measure Z invokes when defending Tessa's curated readings of Meg's journals. Dream compliance describes the point where dream content, waking behavior, and institutional interpretation begin to agree. It is not simply whether a subject obeys in the dream, but whether the dream produces usable evidence of obedience. The term crystallizes Sitri's central horror: compliance becomes real once the system can document it.

The institutional failure category Meg names in her GE19 confession. A cascade failure is not one mistake but a chain reaction: subject escalation, researcher involvement, compromised logs, and collapsing boundaries. By applying the term to Dream Cohort 3-Beta, Meg admits that the problem spread through the system rather than remaining inside a single subject. The phrase gives Sitri's breakdown a technical name while confirming that the failure has become structural.

A Sitri subject whose dreams organize themselves around liminal spaces, corridors, thresholds, transfer tunnels, and in-between places. Lyra enters the episode as an urban photographer whose aesthetic fixation has become dream architecture. Her case reveals that Sitri's dream maps are not merely symbolic. Some of Lyra's stations appear to correspond to real physical features inside the Institute.

The title concept of GE20 and the governing metaphor of Lyra's case. A cusp is a threshold state, neither fully one thing nor the next. In the episode, Z frames the cusp as the space between episodes, between knowledge states, and between what a subject knows and what the system is teaching them. Lyra's dream pathology literalizes the idea by trapping her at points of almost-arrival.

Elle's new role title inside the Sitri Institute after her alignment. She misstates it as Lead Transformer and Lead Transnational Officer, which makes the promotion feel both official and degraded. The title positions Elle as a guide through transition while also revealing how much of her previous intelligence has been repurposed. Her authority is real enough to bother Meg, but unstable enough to amuse everyone else.

A defined marker in Lyra's threshold dream sequence. Party Row appears as a charged in-between space, part street, part stage, part checkpoint. The name sounds casual, but Meg and Tessa treat it as a measurable dream location with predictable physiological effects. It is one of the first signs that Lyra's recurring spaces may be less imaginary than they look.

The dream setting where Lyra encounters Nyra, Hespa, Elle, June, and Cael in Cusp. Crossroads functions as a truck stop, threshold, and ritual exchange point, where roles matter more than ordinary law. Nyra says this is Crossroads as if the place has its own rules and everyone who matters already knows them. The setting turns liminality into a social system.

The untracked corridors inside the Sitri Institute where Tessa and Meg can speak more freely. Tessa says the service corridors are not monitored like the chambers, which makes them rare spaces outside the main data apparatus. Their name echoes Sitri's service-language while also marking a literal backchannel through the Institute. In Cusp, the corridors become the place where the dream map and the building map begin to overlap.

The recurring station names in Lyra's dreams: Sippar, Kutha, Eanna, Ereshkigal, and Ur. Some are ancient Mesopotamian place names, while others are more ritual, mythic, or temple-coded. Tessa recognizes the sequence as meaningful, and the corridor label IDF CLOSET 51P-PAR reveals Sippar hidden in the physical Institute. The Stations suggest that Lyra's dream transit system is mapping onto a deeper architecture.

Meg's phrase for Lyra's psychological and spatial condition. It names the in-between state where social rules loosen and transformation becomes possible. In Cusp, betwixt and between is not just poetic language; it is a working theory of liminal susceptibility. Lyra's case shows Sitri that the most important changes may happen at the threshold, not at the destination.

Meg's diminished institutional role after her loss of status. June uses the title to remind Meg that she has been demoted while still retaining enough knowledge to be useful. The phrase is corporate, cheerful, and humiliating in precisely the Sitri way. It makes punishment sound like customer service.

A dream-world designation used when Elle and June inspect the vehicle in Lyra's Crossroads sequence. The term turns the dream setting into something quasi-official, as if the vehicle is registered inside a service economy with its own rules. Hospitality Unit sounds bureaucratic, but the scene makes clear that hospitality at Crossroads is not neutral. It is another way the dream converts openness into obligation.

Tessa's phrase for the blank part of Lyra's emotional map, the place where fear and wanting become difficult to separate. The expression evokes old maps marking unknown territory as dangerous or monstrous. In Cusp, the danger is not that the map is empty, but that Sitri is beginning to fill it in. The phrase frames Lyra's liminal dreams as exploration of territory the researchers already know is unsafe.

Cael's term for the roles a subject has played before, resurfacing through the station system. In GE21, each station is described as an echo to a prior role rather than a simple dream location. The phrase suggests that Sitri is not only mapping the present subject, but contacting layers of identity, memory, and ritual function that predate the current experiment. Echo lives make the dream feel less invented than remembered.

One of Lyra's recurring stations. In the dream logic of GE21, Eanna is associated with measuring, ledgers, writing, and marks placed on bodies as records of training. The station feels temple-coded and archival, a place where experience becomes inscription. Eanna makes the station map feel ritual and administrative at the same time.

One of Lyra's recurring stations and the first station Tessa and Meg identify physically inside the Institute.
